BRAD SMITH, CNN ANCHOR: That does it for CNN HEADLINE EXPRESS. I'm Brad Smith. CNN THIS MORNING with Audie Cornish starts right now.

ANNOUNCER: This is CNN breaking news

AUDIE CORNISH, CNN ANCHOR: We are following breaking news right now out of the Middle East, where President Trump says the memorandum of understanding with Iran is over.

DONALD TRUMP, U.S. PRESIDENT: As far as I'm concerned, it's just a waste of time dealing with them.

CORNISH: Good morning, everybody. I'm Audie Cornish, and this is where we begin.

Details out of the NATO summit. Now, the comments this morning from President Trump come after the U.S. military hit more than 80 Iranian targets on Tuesday and, in its biggest move yet, reimposed sanctions on Iranian oil in retaliation for attacks on ships near the Strait of Hormuz.

Iran said it would deliver a, quote, "crushing response." But as far as Trump is concerned, he is done negotiating with Iran.

TRUMP: To me, I think it's over. I don't want to deal with them anymore. They're scum. You know what scum is? They're scum. They're sick people. They're led by sick people. And they're vicious, violent people. And if they had a nuclear weapon, they'd use it.

As far as I'm concerned, it's over. I'll speak to our negotiators. They want to negotiate. They're good people, Steve Witkoff, Jared Kushner. But they have to come back to me. As far as I'm concerned, it's just a waste of time dealing with them.

(END VIDEO CLIP) CORNISH: So, we're bringing in CNN senior White House reporter Kevin Liptak, who is in Turkey for this summit.

Kevin, can you talk about what this means for the state of what was known as a ceasefire, even though it was just a memorandum?

KEVIN LIPTAK, CNN SENIOR WHITE HOUSE CORRESPONDENT: Yes, I think it's clear that President Trump thinks this deal has totally collapsed.

But what that actually means for the negotiations and for the war are a little bit unclear.

You know, the president said that he would allow the negotiators to continue these discussions with Iran to try and get some nuclear concessions. Most accounts suggest that those talks had been very, very slow going, even though they were up against this 60-day deadline.

But the president sort of shrugging that off as a waste of time and really entering this summit furious at Iran for being dishonest negotiators, in his view, because they were firing on those commercial vessels in the Strait of Hormuz.

He called them scum. He said that they were cuckoo and suggesting that he's prepared to resume this conflict. But what that looks like, not particularly clear.

Remember, it was only three weeks ago that the president signed this agreement and said that, if the conflict continued, it would result in economic catastrophe. You know, none of those political pressures on the president to end the war have gone away.

And so, how he proceeds from here at this point, not exactly clear.

We did see last night the president ordering up those retaliatory strikes on Iran. He also pulled back those sanctions waivers that he had put on the sales of Iranian oil.

And you've already seen the Iranians sort of list out the ways that they view the United States having violated this agreement.

And so, this is all, I think, falling apart rather quickly. Although the president does say that there's a glimmer of hope that these negotiations can continue.

You know, it all adds up to a furious President Trump. He was angry at Iran, but he's also angry at NATO. You know, there had been some hope, a glimmer of hope, perhaps, that perhaps a more conciliatory president would come here, because he's in a country that's governed by a friend of his, the Turkish president, Recep Tayyip Erdogan.

He said that that's the only reason that he came to this summit at all. But it was evident, listening to him this morning, that all of his grievances and all of his frustrations at NATO have not waned one bit. Listen to what he said here.

TRUMP: We're having the big meeting in a little while, and I'm going to -- I'm going to relate my -- my problems.

When we wanted them -- we didn't want them badly. But we did say, if you want to join us. And they all said, no. But when they could have, they weren't there for us. And we've been there for them.

We're going to work something out, I think, one way or the other. But we've been treated unfairly. We pay disproportionately.

LIPTAK: So, this is all sort of exactly the scenario that the NATO chief, Mark Rutte, other European leaders had wanted to avoid at the summit.

But it seems pretty clear that President Trump is going to be airing all of these grievances as he's now meeting with NATO leaders behind closed doors -- Audie.

CORNISH: OK. That's Kevin Liptak, speaking to us from Ankara in Turkey.

I want to bring in now Kurt Volker, former U.S. ambassador to NATO.

Thank you so much for being here on what is now turning into an eventful day. The president saying that the ceasefire that they had negotiated, their memorandum of understanding, is over after some attacks on ships in the Strait of Hormuz.

I want to play a bit for the president, for you to hear, and then we'll talk on the other side.

TRUMP: They can talk, but I think they're wasting their time. Frankly, I don't want to waste my time with them. Now, I'll let our wonderful negotiators keep talking if they want, but I don't see it.

CORNISH: OK, Ambassador, you know what it's like to have to keep talking. What does this mean for people who had been tasked with continuing this conversation?

KURT VOLKER, FORMER U.S. AMBASSADOR TO NATO: Right. I think President Trump is just trying to ratchet up the visible frustration; try to put more pressure on the Iranians; see if he can get them back to a situation where they're not attacking anything.

What happened is the Iranians attacked some vessels that were trying to go through the Omani side of the Strait of Hormuz. The Iranians don't want that. They want them to go through Iranian waters, where they can gradually establish a regime of control.

Of course, we don't want that.

But the problem is that we may want it or not want it. But if we are not prepared militarily, physically to defeat the regime and prevent them from doing this sort of thing, this is where this is going to go.

I don't think President Trump really wants to restart the war. He may get forced into it, but I don't think he really wants to. So, he's trying to be tougher now to back up his negotiators, to see whether he can get things back where they were with this MOU a few weeks ago.

CORNISH: Yes. You were asked, I think, just last night about Greenland. And you said that Trump has a kind of routine. He complains, he criticizes. He says people aren't doing enough, and he keeps threats on the table. And I thought, gee, that's kind of applicable to this exact moment.

VOLKER: Yes, it is. No, that is the playbook here. He will do that, and he'll never concede anything.

So, even with Greenland, for example, I don't think he was planning to bring up Greenland at the summit at all. But somebody asked him about it. So, he says, yes, of course, it's still on the table. Of course it should be ours.

And I think that is what he's going to do with the Iranians here, step by step. Keep putting the pressure on about the strait. Keep putting pressure on about the nuclear issue. Try to get to something that he can actually live with, which is no fighting, so that oil prices and gasoline prices stay down.

CORNISH: If the ceasefire is over, Does that mean Israel still has a kind of green light to continue its fight with Hezbollah in Lebanon?

Volker: Well, I think that's a very good point, because the Israelis will certainly take it that way. I think President Trump would like to feel that he's in the driver's seat. And so, if Israel is going to attack or not, he would like to be the one who says so.

But I think the Israelis are going to look at this and say, OK, if anything happens, Hezbollah does anything at all, We have a green light. We feel free to go after them again, now.

CORNISH: It's interesting that this NATO summit is happening -- is happening in Ankara. People look at Turkey, in a lot of ways, as a sort of gateway between the East and the West. And it is at the center of this kind of Gordian knot of international issues, right?

You've got Zelenskyy there, trying to get support in its war against Russia. You've got Turkey asking the U.S. for access to F-35 fighter jets, kind of the most advanced fighter jets, and Israel saying, no, don't give it to them.

It feels like the president actually -- there's a lot of spinning plates here. VOLKER: Yes, well, there are. And just look at the geography. I mean,

if you pull up a map, you've got the Black Sea, Ukraine and Russia to the North. You've got Iran and Iraq to the East. You've got Syria and Lebanon to the South.

Further down, you've got Israel on the other side of the mediterranean.

It is a region that has a lot of issues. And Turkey is a very large country, very capable country, militarily, economically. And they play a role in all of these issues that we care about.

Most of the time, U.S. and Turkish interests are actually aligned, but there are few cases where they diverge. And in all of these cases, it's really important that we are talking to the Turks and working with them, because it could go very badly if we don't.

CORNISH: And we should say, despite their requests, we're seeing reports, CNN reporting now that the defense secretary, Hegseth, has actually canceled his trip to Israel, where he was supposed to sort of soothe concerns about this potential sale.

What are you going to be listening for now? Is it J.D. Vance that has to come out and say technical talks are still going, if the Iran -- Iranians play nice? Is it Hegseth? Kind of what are you expecting to see today?

VOLKER: Well, a couple of things. One of them, let's not forget it's the NATO summit meeting. So, I think it would be important for NATO to say something condemning the Iranian attacks on shipping and supporting freedom of navigation in the Persian Gulf.

Very important that NATO stay aligned with the U.S. as we go through this, even if they're not contributing militarily to show the direction that we want to go in.

The second thing is, I think the Iranians are going to listen to President Trump and try to judge: is this just one more threat that he then winds down a couple of days from now? Or is it going to be something different this time?

They're going to be watching that. And in -- and in doing so, I think they're going to stand down on any attacks for a little while.

They had, you know, a quick one just recently, but I think they're going to stand down and see whether the U.S. really wants to escalate this or not. They're probably betting that we don't. And I think things will 00 will be quieter for a while.

CORNISH: OK. That's Ambassador Kurt -- former ambassador Kurt Volker. Thank you so much for your time. Appreciate it.

SCHILLING: Well, what's their policy?

SEAWRIGHT: And this -- and this do-nothing majority.

DOVERE: We're learning that the Democrats have a lot that they're fighting over.

Look, the -- it is not a coincidence that AIPAC and Israel was such an issue in that debate last night. It is an issue that is roiling the Democratic Party right now.

CORNISH: Yes. And Republicans, it's come up here and there. Yes.

DOVERE: And it's an issue. And in Michigan especially, there is a large Arab American population. There's a large Jewish population. There is a lot of different views of what should be done there.

CORNISH: In Israel. I think this is going to be an interesting moment for the party, where someone tries to figure out, how do you thread the needle and navigate a conversation around this that is bedeviling both parties?

DOVERE: To name a successor. And even though people keep saying to him, no, no, no, no, no, he is saying, yes, I can keep doing this, right.

CORNISH: And in the meantime you've got the former Maine state Senate president, Troy Jackson, the first person to officially explore a run. I think we have a couple of other faces and names that we're going to put up later. But in terms of this process, it feels pretty clear that everyone is saying withdraw.

DOVERE: Yes. CORNISH: Why do you think there's a delay here?

ANTJUAN SEAWRIGHT, DEMOCRATIC STRATEGIST: Take everything off the table. He needs -- he needs to withdraw. Start there.

Number two, I always --

CORNISH: Well, like, the clock's a ticking, you know?

SEAWRIGHT: I always believe in the old southern carpenter's rule when it comes to candidates. And I've always believed this throughout my 20 years in this business. Measure three times, cut once. It seems as if that this whole style versus substance conversation that is wrecking our party right now is leading to candidates like this that are tapping into the angry wing of our party, who are frustrated, rightly so, because things are not happening at the pace they wanted to. But yet we're not taking the time to vet candidates.

I credit the Senate Democratic leadership for recruiting Roy Cooper and Janet Mills. They were two of our top recruits this cycle. I think Cooper is going to do well in North Carolina. I also think Janet Mills would have done Maine -- done well in Maine because she's connected to the community. But that is --

DOVERE: She didn't do well in Maine, right? She didn't do well with the voters. Which is a separate conversation whether Platner --

SEAWRIGHT: Well, Isaac, I think she did not do well because there was this big push by those who were angry and not necessarily informed to push in. We need to be anti the establishment.

CORNISH: You mean the voter?

SEAWRIGHT: And that has always been led by Bernie Sanders, the independent senator from Vermont, inserting himself in these primaries. And as a result, when it comes to competitive seats, it does not play out well. Look at Fetterman in Pennsylvania. Look at Tulsi Gabbard and others.

CORNISH: Well, let me let others jump in here because we have a bunch of things to get to, specifically who's going to run next. And to your point, why that matters. If that person is going to be more moderate or not.

Before I come to you, Terry, I'm very sorry. I think we should hear from voters and hear some of them.

UNIDENTIFIED FEMALE: The stakes are so high. If we don't flip the Senate, the Epstein class wins. They win.

UNIDENTIFIED FEMALE: I definitely wouldn't vote for him. So, yes, get out of the race.

UNIDENTIFIED MALE: The best thing we can do is look at the Democratic socialist alternative. At this point, it is pretty apparent that everyone in the Republican and Democratic field does not have the interest in the American -- of the American people in mind.

CORNISH: Check out for me there, young man saying Republicans and Democrats don't have us in mind and looking to a populist answer. And the woman at the start mentioning the Epstein class, which I think that kind of voter is out there.

CORNISH: What do you hear, especially that last person who is not necessarily going to just default to Collins because Platner has problems.

TERRY SCHILLING, PRESIDENT, American PRINCIPLES PROJECT: Well, look, I've said this before. I think that elections have to be future oriented because voters are future oriented.

SCHILLING: They want to know what you're going to do to help them.

Look, what was in Graham Platner's past was all very well known to the people that recruited him. And the party activists that did recruit him said that none of those things that they found, the Reddit post, the Nazi tattoo, all that stuff, none of it should keep him from the nomination.

CORNISH: Yes. They were a Democratic Party but socialists.

DOVERE: But, importantly, no, the --

CORNISH: Well, I'm sorry, can we talk about recruitment for a second?

CORNISH: That means something here. Who recruited him? And is this a lesson learned for the people who are out there saying the parties aren't doing it too well. It's time for other people to step in and do recruiting.

DOVERE: The people who recruited him were people from -- who were not from Maine, for the most part. And those are the same people who are now saying that they get to decide what happens here. One of the top consultants for Platner was in Europe over the last couple days. He is now flying to Maine to help the process that they think that they are going to dictate of who gets to be Platner's replacement.

And again, I do think it's an interesting choice to say that this man, who is accused of sexual assault, now gets to put his will onto the party. SCHILLING: Can I just -- my main point was, is that all that stuff was

already well known. But what changed here, I think, is what these people are seeing on camera, where the voters are saying, we can't support him anymore. And it's not so much with the allegations. I think that just the dynamics of the race have changed. And I think what's worrisome, if I was a Democrat, what I would be worried about right now is it seems that more and more these Democratic Party primaries are no longer primaries and elections, they're just suggestion boxes. And the elites really don't like suggestions.

CORNISH: Let me talk about the suggestion, because now we have to figure out who is next. What is the process? You've got some people jockeying for position. But in the meantime, you've got them just saying basically, beat Susan Collins. We've got a couple names there. Most of them are people who hold state office or have run for state office. But here's a sample of who's talking.

TROY JACKSON, FORMER MAINE SENATE PRESIDENT: Short timeline. Obviously money and all that is going to be a challenge. But I definitely think it's doable because the message, the movement is right and that's what's important.

SHENNA BELLOWS, MAINE SECRETARY OF STATE: I think it's really important that as many Democratic voters have a voice in this process when Graham Platner withdraws.

DR. NIRAV SHAH (D), FORMER MAINE GOVERNOR CANDIDATE: The stakes are incredibly high. We have an obligation, as -- in service of not just Mainers, but in service of the entire country to defeat Senator Collins this upcoming November.

CORNISH: And working backwards, that was Dr. Nirav Shah, former Maine governor candidate, Shenna Bellows, the secretary of state, and Troy Jackson, former Maine Senate president.

For people who might have some trauma around the Biden dropping out of the race and the air quotes process that followed, there are a lot of questions. Should the party have a pop-up convention on the weekend of the 25th, which is the deadline to choose a nominee, should they have a statewide caucus, basically redo the primary election?

Antjuan, what do you think they should do?

SEAWRIGHT: I think we should follow the process.

CORNISH: There is no process.

SEAWRIGHT: Well, that's not true. There is a process in place to put a nominee into place. And I think that's what happened in 2024, regardless of how you -- how you -- whether you agree with how Kamala Harris became my party's nominee. I think we have to follow process.

CORNISH: What is the process? I'm missing something.

SEAWRIGHT: Maine has a process in place to replace a person on their ballot. I think it's like a party pick kind of situation.

SEAWRIGHT: It's a party pick because there is a process in place. There are process in place all across the country for these emergency situations that happen.

I think we need to follow that. That's number one.

Number two, I also don't want to give Republicans a slot here in this moment because they have just as corrupt candidates, from Paxton and others who are on the ballot, but they do not govern themselves the same way when it comes to their issues and Democratic issues. And I think there's a lesson for Democrats to learn in these moments.

Look at Ken Paxton on July 4th. And now we're looking at Ken Paxton. I mean we're looking at our candidate in Maine. There's a lesson about how these things are covered and how we talk about them within the party.

DOVERE: But the lesson for some Democrats was what was going on until Monday with Platner, which is to say all of those things that are supposed to be problems we're going to say aren't problems and we should go forward here. And that is part of how the Democrats got to this place.

And it's a difference in how Democrats and Republicans operate for sure, whether it's Ken Paxton or Max Miller or there are a lot of -- or Donald Trump, there are a lot of people --

SEAWRIGHT: That's right.

DOVERE: Who are Republicans who have had a lot of accusations made against them that are pretty serious.

SCHILLING: But again, the reason that Paxton's succeeding is because he has a compelling policy agenda. He won over the voters. He had 130 --

SCHILLING: Antjuan, he had $130 million in party money spent against him, and he crushed it by -- won by double digits.

SEAWRIGHT: Terry, Terry, Terry. Terry --

SCHILLING: It's about -- it's about substance. It's about policy. It's about how you're going to protect people and make their lives better, period. SEAWRIGHT: Terry, Terry, respectfully, you're going to ignore the

SCHILLING: There's no -- there's no rape allegations. There's no Nazi tattoos. There's no reckless (ph) --

SEAWRIGHT: You -- no, no, you're going to -- you're going to ignore the moral conversation around Ken Paxton?

SCHILLING: No, no. No, here's the moral --

SEAWRIGHT: You're going to ignore the moral conversation around Max Miller beating his wife in front of his kids.

SCHILLING: Here's the moral conversation. Here's the -- here's the moral conversation in a democracy.

SEAWRIGHT: You can't -- you can't come to us and say that these things --

SCHILLING: In a democracy voters get to decide.

SEAWRIGHT: Somehow or another we should ignore those things because of somehow or another some right-wing policy issues --

SCHILLING: In a democracy, voters get to choose their party leader (ph).

CORNISH: All right, I'm going to bring it back to a female victim. I want to just repeat this before we go. This is Lyndsey Fifield, who is the accuser against Graham Platner, talking about violence and abuse. And she says something that I think we should remember going forward as we're trying to decide politically, as a political class, what matters in a man's history. And she says, "the things that he does in private, in his relationships with women, the things that he has done to women, coercive, control, lying, deception, abuse, and really horrible things that we know about him," she says, "we know that you cannot be that way in private and not have it translate into how you would govern."

You guys, stay with us. We're going to come back.

Right now I want to show you live images from New York because they are actually trying to stabilize a high rise. Crews are working overnight to shore up the weakest parts of a building.

SCOTT JENNINGS, CNN SENIOR POLITICAL COMMENTATOR: We talk for about 17 minutes. You know, he talked a lot about the situation with Iran. He talked a little bit about Ukraine and what's going on in Europe. I'd gotten a few text messages from him, but I hadn't spoken to him.

So, it was good to hear his voice. His voice sounded strong and sounded like he was keeping up with the news to me.

CORNISH: OK, Senator Mitch McConnell has been hospitalized for three weeks. Still unclear why or when he'll be released. And yet some MAGA voices demanding, quote, "proof of life." Here's an example. Laura Loomer posting, "high level source close to the White House tells me, quote, 'Mitch McConnell is officially brain dead and he's not coming back'."

Again, that is Laura Loomer on X saying that.

And then this is a former senior adviser for Kamala Harris' campaign, Mike Nellis.

MIKE NELLIS, FORMER SENIOR ADVISER FOR KAMALA HARRIS' CAMPAIGN: If Scott Jennings and Thune and everybody else could have a 20 minute phone call with Mitch McConnell, he should be able to shoot a 60 second proof of life video to show us that he's OK.

I think they know the deadline for a special election is August 3rd. If he's dead or if he's on a ventilator, if he's not able to do his job, he should resign right now. They should have a special election.

CORNISH: So, you have a lot of Senate Republicans coming out. We're going to just show some on screen, Isaac, all saying, I talked to him, I talked to him, I talked to him for 20 minutes, I talked to him for 45 minutes.

We heard -- we're hearing these pundits talk not just about his potential health, but what they think the issue is, a special election deadline.

DOVERE: Yes. Look, Audie, if you were -- hadn't been on air for three weeks and there was no answers where you had gone, probably CNN would be interested to know what was going on with you.

CORNISH: Yes, although Reddit would say thank you.

DOVERE: Same thing with -- sure. Maybe.

DOVERE: I think the same would be true of me probably for you guys, too.

DOVERE: Mitch McConnell is entitled to private information about his health, but he is a public official. He is the taxpayers of Kentucky and of the federal government -- and of the country, because he's a federal employee, are entitled to know what's going on.

SEAWRIGHT: Isaac, this is the second Republican who we've had these medical mishaps, if you will, missing in action. And I just think that from the party who claims transparency and accountability, we should just know more when these situations pop up. We had a member of Congress who was missing in action for months.

SEAWRIGHT: And we didn't hear anything. And we later learned depression. Now we have Senator McConnell. If this were Democrats, Republicans would be wetting their diapers about accountability and responsibility.

DOVERE: If these are -- if these are important jobs, as they are --

SCHILLING: Well, it's just interesting because I am old enough to remember the Biden years where we had our secretary of defense absolutely AWOL, went out for a surgery for, I think, three weeks. We had the president of the United States obviously suffering from some type of a mental affliction and suffering, dementia (ph).

SEAWRIGHT: OK, do you have medical proof of that?

SCHILLING: Well, I saw it on TV. I saw it would my own eyes.

CORNISH: Well, hold on. Let me get -- let me bring you one other thing.

SCHILLING: But we were gaslit and told that he was at the top of his game.

CORNISH: We're going to give the last word to Lloyd Austin, who you just mentioned.

SCHILLING: Here we go.

CORNISH: Because back in 2024, he answered questions about this. And this is a great way to end the show. Here is the lesson he took away from being private about your health and not telling the public.

LLOYD AUSTIN, FORMER BIDEN SECRETARY OF DEFENSE: I don't think it's news that I'm a pretty private guy. I never liked burdening others with my problems. It's just not my way.

But I've learned from this experience. Taking this kind of job means losing some of the privacy that most of us expect. The American people have a right to know if their leaders are facing health challenges that might affect their ability to perform their duties, even temporarily.

CORNISH: And later on, testified before Congress that there would be changes made to notify the chain of command, as well as the public. There is a way for Congress and lawmakers to deal with this. They have not chosen to.

I want to thank you guys for being here. I want to thank you for waking up with me. I'm Audie Cornish, and the headlines are next.

Trump: Memorandum of Understanding with Iran is 'Over'; Michigan Senate Candidates Clash Over Future of Democratic Party. Aired 6-6:30a ET

Aired July 08, 2026 - 06:00   ET

THIS IS A RUSH TRANSCRIPT. THIS COPY MAY NOT BE IN ITS FINAL FORM AND MAY BE UPDATED.

BRAD SMITH, CNN ANCHOR: That does it for CNN HEADLINE EXPRESS. I'm Brad Smith. CNN THIS MORNING with Audie Cornish starts right now.

ANNOUNCER: This is CNN breaking news

AUDIE CORNISH, CNN ANCHOR: We are following breaking news right now out of the Middle East, where President Trump says the memorandum of understanding with Iran is over.

DONALD TRUMP, U.S. PRESIDENT: As far as I'm concerned, it's just a waste of time dealing with them.

CORNISH: Good morning, everybody. I'm Audie Cornish, and this is where we begin.

Details out of the NATO summit. Now, the comments this morning from President Trump come after the U.S. military hit more than 80 Iranian targets on Tuesday and, in its biggest move yet, reimposed sanctions on Iranian oil in retaliation for attacks on ships near the Strait of Hormuz.

Iran said it would deliver a, quote, "crushing response." But as far as Trump is concerned, he is done negotiating with Iran.

TRUMP: To me, I think it's over. I don't want to deal with them anymore. They're scum. You know what scum is? They're scum. They're sick people. They're led by sick people. And they're vicious, violent people. And if they had a nuclear weapon, they'd use it.

As far as I'm concerned, it's over. I'll speak to our negotiators. They want to negotiate. They're good people, Steve Witkoff, Jared Kushner. But they have to come back to me. As far as I'm concerned, it's just a waste of time dealing with them.

(END VIDEO CLIP) CORNISH: So, we're bringing in CNN senior White House reporter Kevin Liptak, who is in Turkey for this summit.

Kevin, can you talk about what this means for the state of what was known as a ceasefire, even though it was just a memorandum?

KEVIN LIPTAK, CNN SENIOR WHITE HOUSE CORRESPONDENT: Yes, I think it's clear that President Trump thinks this deal has totally collapsed.

But what that actually means for the negotiations and for the war are a little bit unclear.

You know, the president said that he would allow the negotiators to continue these discussions with Iran to try and get some nuclear concessions. Most accounts suggest that those talks had been very, very slow going, even though they were up against this 60-day deadline.

But the president sort of shrugging that off as a waste of time and really entering this summit furious at Iran for being dishonest negotiators, in his view, because they were firing on those commercial vessels in the Strait of Hormuz.

He called them scum. He said that they were cuckoo and suggesting that he's prepared to resume this conflict. But what that looks like, not particularly clear.

Remember, it was only three weeks ago that the president signed this agreement and said that, if the conflict continued, it would result in economic catastrophe. You know, none of those political pressures on the president to end the war have gone away.

And so, how he proceeds from here at this point, not exactly clear.

We did see last night the president ordering up those retaliatory strikes on Iran. He also pulled back those sanctions waivers that he had put on the sales of Iranian oil.

And you've already seen the Iranians sort of list out the ways that they view the United States having violated this agreement.

And so, this is all, I think, falling apart rather quickly. Although the president does say that there's a glimmer of hope that these negotiations can continue.

You know, it all adds up to a furious President Trump. He was angry at Iran, but he's also angry at NATO. You know, there had been some hope, a glimmer of hope, perhaps, that perhaps a more conciliatory president would come here, because he's in a country that's governed by a friend of his, the Turkish president, Recep Tayyip Erdogan.

He said that that's the only reason that he came to this summit at all. But it was evident, listening to him this morning, that all of his grievances and all of his frustrations at NATO have not waned one bit. Listen to what he said here.

TRUMP: We're having the big meeting in a little while, and I'm going to -- I'm going to relate my -- my problems.

When we wanted them -- we didn't want them badly. But we did say, if you want to join us. And they all said, no. But when they could have, they weren't there for us. And we've been there for them.

We're going to work something out, I think, one way or the other. But we've been treated unfairly. We pay disproportionately.

LIPTAK: So, this is all sort of exactly the scenario that the NATO chief, Mark Rutte, other European leaders had wanted to avoid at the summit.

But it seems pretty clear that President Trump is going to be airing all of these grievances as he's now meeting with NATO leaders behind closed doors -- Audie.

CORNISH: OK. That's Kevin Liptak, speaking to us from Ankara in Turkey.

I want to bring in now Kurt Volker, former U.S. ambassador to NATO.

Thank you so much for being here on what is now turning into an eventful day. The president saying that the ceasefire that they had negotiated, their memorandum of understanding, is over after some attacks on ships in the Strait of Hormuz.

I want to play a bit for the president, for you to hear, and then we'll talk on the other side.

TRUMP: They can talk, but I think they're wasting their time. Frankly, I don't want to waste my time with them. Now, I'll let our wonderful negotiators keep talking if they want, but I don't see it.

CORNISH: OK, Ambassador, you know what it's like to have to keep talking. What does this mean for people who had been tasked with continuing this conversation?

KURT VOLKER, FORMER U.S. AMBASSADOR TO NATO: Right. I think President Trump is just trying to ratchet up the visible frustration; try to put more pressure on the Iranians; see if he can get them back to a situation where they're not attacking anything.

What happened is the Iranians attacked some vessels that were trying to go through the Omani side of the Strait of Hormuz. The Iranians don't want that. They want them to go through Iranian waters, where they can gradually establish a regime of control.

Of course, we don't want that.

But the problem is that we may want it or not want it. But if we are not prepared militarily, physically to defeat the regime and prevent them from doing this sort of thing, this is where this is going to go.

I don't think President Trump really wants to restart the war. He may get forced into it, but I don't think he really wants to. So, he's trying to be tougher now to back up his negotiators, to see whether he can get things back where they were with this MOU a few weeks ago.

CORNISH: Yes. You were asked, I think, just last night about Greenland. And you said that Trump has a kind of routine. He complains, he criticizes. He says people aren't doing enough, and he keeps threats on the table. And I thought, gee, that's kind of applicable to this exact moment.

VOLKER: Yes, it is. No, that is the playbook here. He will do that, and he'll never concede anything.

So, even with Greenland, for example, I don't think he was planning to bring up Greenland at the summit at all. But somebody asked him about it. So, he says, yes, of course, it's still on the table. Of course it should be ours.

And I think that is what he's going to do with the Iranians here, step by step. Keep putting the pressure on about the strait. Keep putting pressure on about the nuclear issue. Try to get to something that he can actually live with, which is no fighting, so that oil prices and gasoline prices stay down.

CORNISH: If the ceasefire is over, Does that mean Israel still has a kind of green light to continue its fight with Hezbollah in Lebanon?

Volker: Well, I think that's a very good point, because the Israelis will certainly take it that way. I think President Trump would like to feel that he's in the driver's seat. And so, if Israel is going to attack or not, he would like to be the one who says so.

But I think the Israelis are going to look at this and say, OK, if anything happens, Hezbollah does anything at all, We have a green light. We feel free to go after them again, now.

CORNISH: It's interesting that this NATO summit is happening -- is happening in Ankara. People look at Turkey, in a lot of ways, as a sort of gateway between the East and the West. And it is at the center of this kind of Gordian knot of international issues, right?

You've got Zelenskyy there, trying to get support in its war against Russia. You've got Turkey asking the U.S. for access to F-35 fighter jets, kind of the most advanced fighter jets, and Israel saying, no, don't give it to them.

It feels like the president actually -- there's a lot of spinning plates here. VOLKER: Yes, well, there are. And just look at the geography. I mean,

if you pull up a map, you've got the Black Sea, Ukraine and Russia to the North. You've got Iran and Iraq to the East. You've got Syria and Lebanon to the South.

Further down, you've got Israel on the other side of the mediterranean.

It is a region that has a lot of issues. And Turkey is a very large country, very capable country, militarily, economically. And they play a role in all of these issues that we care about.

Most of the time, U.S. and Turkish interests are actually aligned, but there are few cases where they diverge. And in all of these cases, it's really important that we are talking to the Turks and working with them, because it could go very badly if we don't.

CORNISH: And we should say, despite their requests, we're seeing reports, CNN reporting now that the defense secretary, Hegseth, has actually canceled his trip to Israel, where he was supposed to sort of soothe concerns about this potential sale.

What are you going to be listening for now? Is it J.D. Vance that has to come out and say technical talks are still going, if the Iran -- Iranians play nice? Is it Hegseth? Kind of what are you expecting to see today?

VOLKER: Well, a couple of things. One of them, let's not forget it's the NATO summit meeting. So, I think it would be important for NATO to say something condemning the Iranian attacks on shipping and supporting freedom of navigation in the Persian Gulf.

Very important that NATO stay aligned with the U.S. as we go through this, even if they're not contributing militarily to show the direction that we want to go in.

The second thing is, I think the Iranians are going to listen to President Trump and try to judge: is this just one more threat that he then winds down a couple of days from now? Or is it going to be something different this time?

They're going to be watching that. And in -- and in doing so, I think they're going to stand down on any attacks for a little while.

They had, you know, a quick one just recently, but I think they're going to stand down and see whether the U.S. really wants to escalate this or not. They're probably betting that we don't. And I think things will 00 will be quieter for a while.

CORNISH: OK. That's Ambassador Kurt -- former ambassador Kurt Volker. Thank you so much for your time. Appreciate it.

VOLKER: It is appreciated (ph). CORNISH: And coming up on CNN THIS MORNING, we are monitoring the situation in New York. Construction crews are working to stabilize a high rise.

UNIDENTIFIED MALE: It was crazy. And everybody -- everybody was panicking.

CORNISH: Why officials are confident that they can keep a building from buckling.

Plus, Democrats still trying to answer for Israel. Now it's coming to a head on Michigan's debate stage.

And why Senator Mitch McConnell's team is being secretive about his health. Questions being raised about whether they owe it to voters to explain what's going on.

UNIDENTIFIED MALE: Mitch McConnell talked to the GOP leaders on the phone. I feel like we have a "Weekend at Bernie's" situation going on right here.

ABDUL EL-SAYED (D), MICHIGAN SENATORIAL CANDIDATE: If you want somebody who's electable, the last three polls in a row show that I'm the most electable Democrat in November. Why? Because you got to actually fight for something.

REP. HALEY STEVENS (D-MI), MICHIGAN SENATORIAL CANDIDATE: I worked really hard to make sure that Kamala Harris was going to get elected, and that Donald Trump would be stopped. And my opponent did nothing.

CORNISH: You're looking at the last two candidates remaining in the Democratic primary for Senate in Michigan. They were in a heated debate on Tuesday night. This is ahead of their primary, which is on August 4th.

Abdul El-Sayed and Congresswoman Haley Stevens are fighting to replace retiring Senator Gary Peters. The race is critical for Democrats, who are hoping to flip the Senate in November. It's also exposing a rift in the national party between more moderate establishment party forces and a progressive insurgency. (BEGIN VIDEO CLIP)

EL-SAYED: Chuck Schumer desperately wants one of us to be the next senator, and it's not me. So, if you want your politics dictated to you by AIPAC or Chuck Schumer, then I'm not your guy.

STEVENS: There are many who want to make this run for U.S. Senate about Washington, D.C., insider deals and what's going on with our party leadership. Friends, this is about the future of Michigan.

CORNISH: So, joining me now in the group chat, Isaac Dovere, CNN senior reporter; Terry Schilling, president of the American Principles Project; and Antjuan Seawright, Democratic strategist.

Isaac, I want to start with you, because there's a handful of Senate races were all watching very closely. And yet you call this one the apex for Democrats? Why?

EDWARD-ISAAC DOVERE, CNN SENIOR CORRESPONDENT: Yes. Well, look, this is really where you see, in this primary, especially now with Mallory McMorrow dropping out at the beginning of the week, this just playing out in full contrast of the different things that are going on in the Democratic Party, this fight that is happening. The fight for both the hearts of Democrats and the minds of Democrats, who they want to believe they are.

Is it a bipartisan, workhorse version of things, the way that Haley Stevens supporters sell forward, or is it this progressive fighter model that that that Abdul El-Sayed has put himself forward as? And in the minds --

CORNISH: Whether or not those things are even at odds.

CORNISH: I'm sure the moderates would say, like, we're fighting up a storm.

DOVERE: They would. And there's a lot that overlaps in terms of policy for both of them.

And then it's also about the minds, where they're thinking, this is a race. There is no way that anybody really can see that Democrats can take the majority of the Senate, which is already a stretch, if they lose this seat in Michigan.

And so, part of what even played out on the debate stage, what you're playing before we started is this conversation of who will be the strongest candidate to go up against Mike Rogers, former congressman who very nearly won the Senate seat against Elissa Slotkin in 2024.

CORNISH: OK. I want to play for you guys a little snippet of the debate, just so the audience can hear a little bit about what was talked about. Here's an exchange they had about -- even though it's the state of Michigan -- U.S. policy on Israel.

EL-SAYED: For too long, our foreign policy has been handed to us by the likes of the state of Israel and AIPAC, who has made sure that Democrats and Republicans are doing their bidding.

I don't take that money. They are spending against me, because they've called me the most dangerous candidate for the U.S.-Israel relationship.

STEVENS: Well, Abdul, I would say no one is afraid of you. And in fact, the GOP is spending thousands of dollars to prop up your campaign, because they think it -- they will make it easier for Mike Rogers to win if you are the nominee.

EL-SAYED: Well, you know, if Congresswoman Stevens makes it or Mike Rogers wins, either way, Israel will win. AIPAC is perfectly fine with either of my two opponents, because they know that they will have a comfortable, reliable vote in the U.S. Senate.

CORNISH: I wanted to play that, one, because the "no one is afraid of you" was like, kind of quite the dig. And I want to be clear for the audience, it's not like we're verifying the claims they're making in this debate.

Antjuan, what do you see in sort of how they're going at each other? Is it reflective of anything or is this a Michigan race?

ANTJUAN SEAWRIGHT, DEMOCRATIC STRATEGIST: This is a race solely about Michigan. But I will add, this is a race solely about the future of our country. Take Michigan out of it.

I think where there's no pathway for the majority and for us to have the majority in the Senate without us maintaining the state of Michigan in our column.

I would also add, we continue to have these conversations of style versus substance in our primaries. And quite frankly, I think it's doing a disservice to the overall goal. And that's to win in November.

Say what you want about the Republicans, but they seem to be united after primaries. We have a problem doing that. And I hope that Michigan is not one of those examples.

The truth of the matter is Michigan is a battleground state in 2028. And so, we have to set the tone in 2026. That's why this primary is so important.

SEAWRIGHT: I think people forget that there were strong divisions within Michigan in the 2024 election cycle that caused us not to put Michigan in the winning column for Democrats.

CORNISH: Are you guys united?

TERRY SCHILLING, PRESIDENT, AMERICAN PRINCIPLES PROJECT: Look, yes, no. Mike Rogers, the party's aligned behind him.

I think the big thing that I took away from that debate last night is that it's a lot of style over substance, right? They're not talking about exciting policies that are going to transform Americans' lives. They're talking over about campaign disclosures, financial disclosures. They're talking about who can win, who's best seated to win. Well, the reality is --

CORNISH: Well, maybe because the policies are actually quite similar.

SEAWRIGHT: I disagree with they're not talking about issues, because I think that both candidates are talking about the affordability crisis that Republicans have created. They're both talking about this.

CORNISH: You're saying they're not clashing on it, right? Like they basically --

SEAWRIGHT: Well, that's the point.

SCHILLING: I'm saying, actually, that they're -- they don't have exciting plans.

If I had a plan that I actually thought was going to transform people's lives, I'm not going to talk about my campaign disclosures or my candidate's campaign disclosures. I'm going to talk -- or certainly not AIPAC.

SEAWRIGHT: I think that's where you may be wrong. With all due respect, Terry, they are talking about the affordability issue. They all talk -- they're all talking about health care. They're all talking about the 100,000 manufacturing jobs that have been lost under this administration.

SCHILLING: Well, what's their policy?

SEAWRIGHT: And this -- and this do-nothing majority.

SCHILLING: All right. They talked a lot about it. What was their policy on --

SEAWRIGHT: Costs, care.

SCHILLING: No, how are they going to solve those things?

SEAWRIGHT: Costs, lowering costs.

SCHILLING: How are they going to lower costs.

SEAWRIGHT: For -- dealing with the health care crisis. The 18.2 million Americans --

SCHILLING: How are they dealing with the -- with the crisis?

SEAWRIGHT: Well, they're going to -- I just told you. We're going to pass a --

SCHILLING: Raising taxes?

SEAWRIGHT: No. We're going to pass those ACA subsidies that Republicans refused to pass to make sure that premiums --

SCHILLING: So, raise taxes.

CORNISH: So, hold on one second.

SEAWRIGHT: Premiums, co-pays and deductibles do not rise.

CORNISH: You guys right now are having the argument we plan to listen to in 2028.

SCHILLING: Yes, that's right.

CORNISH: Don't want to jump the gun.

SEAWRIGHT: No, this is a 2026 argument.

CORNISH: We're still -- well, I want to talk about the primaries, because we are not there yet. The argument you're talking about is the argument they have to make in a general. This is what we do different from Republicans.

It's getting there. The journey is the destination right now. And what are we learning?

DOVERE: We're learning that the Democrats have a lot that they're fighting over.

Look, the -- it is not a coincidence that AIPAC and Israel was such an issue in that debate last night. It is an issue that is roiling the Democratic Party right now.

CORNISH: Yes. And Republicans, it's come up here and there. Yes.

DOVERE: And it's an issue. And in Michigan especially, there is a large Arab American population. There's a large Jewish population. There is a lot of different views of what should be done there.

El-Sayed has said over and over that he is anti-AIPAC. And he said to me in an interview I did with him last week that the -- the only way that anybody could support Israel is if there's money supporting them.

There is a lot of AIPAC-related money that's going to Haley Stevens.

But it's also an issue that that El-Sayed has said speaks to deeper progressive values --

CORNISH: Yes. Let me --

DOVERE: -- of where people are. And that's not what Stevens thinks. And it's not clear where voters are on this.

CORNISH: It is not. And let me pause on that particular conversation for this reason.

Rahm Emanuel, someone who has been out there, former Democratic operative, is planning to make a speech and talk about Israel. I think this is going to be an interesting --

CORNISH: In Israel. I think this is going to be an interesting moment for the party, where someone tries to figure out, how do you thread the needle and navigate a conversation around this that is bedeviling both parties?

So, let's pause on this, because we are definitely going to come back to it.

And in the meantime, I just want to give you guys an update on a couple of things. The president's lawsuit against "The Washington Post" thrown out. Why a judge said the Trump administration did not provide enough evidence.

CORNISH: Argentina's epic comeback to keep their World Cup dreams alive.

CORNISH: It is now 28 minutes past the hour. This is your morning roundup.

Now I want to show you right now live images. Construction crews working through the night, because they need to shore up a high rise in midtown Manhattan.

UNIDENTIFIED MALE: We're getting reports from the construction that two columns buckled on the 21st floor.

UNIDENTIFIED MALE: Construction company has done a roll call. All construction workers are accounted for at this time.

CORNISH: Did you hear that word, "buckled"? The building was evacuated after two columns bent on Tuesday.

This is the former headquarters of Pfizer, which is being converted into apartments. Several other buildings were also cleared out as a precaution.

UNIDENTIFIED MALE: It was a disaster the way they were -- they were running out of the hotel. It was crazy. And everybody -- everybody was panicking. A lot of people was [SIC] panicking.

CORNISH: And the New York City building commissioner says crews at the time were trying to add 11 new floors to the building, which already had 22 floors.

segments.title: CNN.com - Transcripts
segments.text:
Return to Transcripts main page

Trump's Done Dealing with Iran; Platner Considering Best Path Forward; Questions Over Mitch McConnell's Health. Aired 6:30-7a ET

Aired July 08, 2026 - 06:30   ET

THIS IS A RUSH TRANSCRIPT. THIS COPY MAY NOT BE IN ITS FINAL FORM AND MAY BE UPDATED.

AUDIE CORNISH, CNN ANCHOR: City building commissioner says crews at the time were trying to add 11 new floors to the building, which already had 22 floors.

President Trump's $3.8 billion defamation lawsuit against "The Washington Post," now thrown out by a judge. The president's media and technology group brought the suit in 2023 over a "Post" article alleging securities fraud. But a federal judge in Florida ruled that Trump media failed to present evidence of actual malice.

And the World Cup now down to eight teams after a pair of crazy finishes to the round of 16 Tuesday, led by Messi. Defending champion Argentina stunned Egypt, scoring three second half goals to come back from a two-goal deficit. In the meantime, Switzerland took down Colombia in a penalty kick shootout after the game ended in a tie. And it will be the first time the Swiss will have reached the World Cup quarter finals since 1954.

And today the nominations will be announced for the 78th Emmy Awards. "The Pitt" and "Hacks" expected to bring in a number of nods. Yesterday, the Emmy's announced "Law and Order SVU" star Mariska Hargitay will host the show, and she will be the first female host in 15 years. Who knew?

Straight ahead on CNN THIS MORNING ,we're going to continue to follow that breaking news out of the Middle East. That's where the president has just said that the ceasefire with Iran is over. We're going to try and figure out what that means.

UNIDENTIFIED FEMALE: We have a very short window here to pull ourselves together and find somebody.

CORNISH: The Platner problem. In Maine, his top backer now says he should drop out, but do Democrats have enough time to run a successful campaign to beat Senator Susan Collins?

And in the meantime, good morning to Chicago. This is a look at the sunrise over the city's skyline.

CORNISH: So, we continue to follow this breaking news out of the Middle East. President Trump saying that the memorandum of understanding with Iran is over. So that means this truce is uncertain right now.

It follows a flare-up overnight between the U.S. and Iran. The U.S. hitting 80 targets. And Iran promising a crushing response. President Trump, however, says he is done talking.

DONALD TRUMP, PRESIDENT OF THE UNITED STATES: As far as I'm concerned, it's just a waste of time dealing with them. They're liars.

Everyone's agreed, no nuclear weapon. We make a deal. They go outside, talk to the press. They say, we never even talked about it. There's something wrong with them. They're cuckoo.

CORNISH: I'm going to bring in CNN international diplomatic editor Nic Robertson, who is in Ankara.

And, Nic, leading up to this, we had the president talking about the funeral proceedings that had been happening in Iran. You had the technical talks that were supposed to keep going for 60 days. How did this break down? What is known right now?

NIC ROBERTSON, CNN INTERNATIONAL DIPLOMATIC EDITOR: Well, the president did acknowledge that the negotiators do want to keep talking. And I think a lot of people will hold on to that and say, that's a positive right there. And what we've seen in the past is the Qatari and Pakistani mediators who are in the middle of this, working diligently, slowly behind the scenes, one step at a time, trying to talk both sides down, trying to de-escalate at times, getting the original ceasefire, eventually getting that MOU after almost two months of hard diplomatic lifting.

I don't think they're going to want to drop it. But it really does depend on what the president has to say and where he has committed his direction of travel on this. And we've seen that he does want to try to make a deal. But I think putting it in the context of the NATO summit here, you know, the secretary general, Mark Rutte, said he expected it to be a topic of conversation. The president sort of put it on the agenda yesterday before the strikes because he felt NATO nations should have stepped up more to help.

But the picture here is, they really want to focus on Ukraine. And President Zelenskyy is desperate to get more air defense missiles to stop the big, ballistic missiles that are killing people in Ukraine. And over the past few months there's a sense in Europe that the U.S.,

Iran, Israel war has been a distraction, consuming the very resources that Ukraine really needs, missile defense systems, and that this will be a deviation off the track of what NATO really wants to focus on today. There's no doubt it will get discussion. But, of course, here we are talking about it. And really the thrust here is to get on and support Ukraine.

CORNISH: Nic, one other thing, though. When it comes to Ukraine, they've become the leading kind of battlefield experts on asymmetrical warfare and drones. And this is the exact issue the U.S. is dealing with when it comes to Iran. Is there any card to be played, so to speak, by Zelenskyy?

ROBERTSON: Zelenskyy clearly believes that he does have cards to play with the drones, their manufacturer, the long distance drones that are reaching more than a thousand, certainly 2,000 miles inside of Russia, hitting sensitive Russian targets. And we saw President Zelenskyy pretty much when Iran began hitting the United States' friends and allies in the gulf, he went out to Saudi, he went out to others in the gulf and said, we've got these defensive capabilities against drones. Here's how they work. And he signed contracts.

So, I think Zelenskyy, and certainly his European supporters here would recognize that he is at the cutting edge, that drone technology changes quickly on the battlefield.

His country is a battlefield. He has the space, the technology, the people, the equipment to be at the cutting edge. So, he has something to offer. But he needs the money and he needs the resources against these ballistic missiles. And that's, you know, where he -- the card that he's short of. And he's made that very clear just yesterday in his comments here, saying that Europe needs an air defense missile system capability, needs to be able to do it at scale, and quickly.

CORNISH: OK, that's Nic Robertson, speaking to us about the news out of the NATO Summit. You heard him there talking about Ukraine. We're also following reporting about the president saying that his memorandum of understanding is off and his frustrations with Iran.

I want to turn to some domestic politics because the balance of the Senate is in question. Graham Platner's Senate candidacy hanging on by a thread this morning up in Maine. This is after a new sexual assault allegation against the democratic candidate. His original accuser now coming forward, telling CNN's Jake Tapper that Platner was a violent boyfriend.

LYNDSEY FIFIELD, GRAHAM PLATNER ACCUSER: The things that he does in private, in his relationships with women, the things that he has done to women, coercive control, lying, deception, abuse, and really horrible things that we've -- that we've -- that we know about him -- sorry, we know that, that you can't be that way in private and not have it translate into how you would govern.

CORNISH: And Platner is facing widespread calls to drop out of the race from the Democratic Party. That includes one of his biggest supporters, Bernie Sanders. In a statement, Sanders writes, "I have spoken with Graham Platner about the best path forward for Maine. In light of these very serious allegations," Sanders says, "I have recommended that he step down."

The group chat is back.

I think this is one of those stories that is actually in political group chats as more and more information drips out. But let me let people understand what's happening here. I think according to my notes, he has to withdraw by the 13th. And the reason why that's important is the secretary of state cannot allow a new name on the ballot until there's a vacancy. So, this is why everyone is on Platner watch. But how is Platner spending his time?

EDWARD-ISAAC DOVERE, CNN SENIOR REPORTER: Well, he's spending his time huddled in private trying to figure out what to do.

Look, there are two sets of things here.

CORNISH: Well, it sounded like he was busy pressuring the Democratic Party again (ph).

DOVERE: Well, right. I mean that's the thing. And so, thing number one is, a lot of Democrats who were very gung ho about him, until the revelations this week, do need to face their own questions of why everything else was allowed with Platner and they were for him. The Nazi tattoo, the Reddit post with all the political views saying black people don't tip, the previous allegations from women that were all waved off and said it's not enough to be an issue for him, but that this, all of a sudden, was.

The other thing is, when it comes to the -- replacing Platner on the ballot, perhaps, we have a situation here where Platner is holding -- he is saying that after being accused of sexual assault and abusing women, that he now has the right to have some control that he forces on the Democratic Party.

CORNISH: To name a successor.

DOVERE: To name a successor. And even though people keep saying to him, no, no, no, no, no, he is saying, yes, I can keep doing this, right.

CORNISH: And in the meantime you've got the former Maine state Senate president, Troy Jackson, the first person to officially explore a run. I think we have a couple of other faces and names that we're going to put up later. But in terms of this process, it feels pretty clear that everyone is saying withdraw.

DOVERE: Yes. CORNISH: Why do you think there's a delay here?

ANTJUAN SEAWRIGHT, DEMOCRATIC STRATEGIST: Take everything off the table. He needs -- he needs to withdraw. Start there.

Number two, I always --

CORNISH: Well, like, the clock's a ticking, you know?

SEAWRIGHT: I always believe in the old southern carpenter's rule when it comes to candidates. And I've always believed this throughout my 20 years in this business. Measure three times, cut once. It seems as if that this whole style versus substance conversation that is wrecking our party right now is leading to candidates like this that are tapping into the angry wing of our party, who are frustrated, rightly so, because things are not happening at the pace they wanted to. But yet we're not taking the time to vet candidates.

I credit the Senate Democratic leadership for recruiting Roy Cooper and Janet Mills. They were two of our top recruits this cycle. I think Cooper is going to do well in North Carolina. I also think Janet Mills would have done Maine -- done well in Maine because she's connected to the community. But that is --

DOVERE: She didn't do well in Maine, right? She didn't do well with the voters. Which is a separate conversation whether Platner --

SEAWRIGHT: Well, Isaac, I think she did not do well because there was this big push by those who were angry and not necessarily informed to push in. We need to be anti the establishment.

CORNISH: You mean the voter?

SEAWRIGHT: And that has always been led by Bernie Sanders, the independent senator from Vermont, inserting himself in these primaries. And as a result, when it comes to competitive seats, it does not play out well. Look at Fetterman in Pennsylvania. Look at Tulsi Gabbard and others.

CORNISH: Well, let me let others jump in here because we have a bunch of things to get to, specifically who's going to run next. And to your point, why that matters. If that person is going to be more moderate or not.

Before I come to you, Terry, I'm very sorry. I think we should hear from voters and hear some of them.

UNIDENTIFIED FEMALE: The stakes are so high. If we don't flip the Senate, the Epstein class wins. They win.

UNIDENTIFIED FEMALE: I definitely wouldn't vote for him. So, yes, get out of the race.

UNIDENTIFIED MALE: The best thing we can do is look at the Democratic socialist alternative. At this point, it is pretty apparent that everyone in the Republican and Democratic field does not have the interest in the American -- of the American people in mind.

CORNISH: Check out for me there, young man saying Republicans and Democrats don't have us in mind and looking to a populist answer. And the woman at the start mentioning the Epstein class, which I think that kind of voter is out there.

CORNISH: What do you hear, especially that last person who is not necessarily going to just default to Collins because Platner has problems.

TERRY SCHILLING, PRESIDENT, American PRINCIPLES PROJECT: Well, look, I've said this before. I think that elections have to be future oriented because voters are future oriented.

SCHILLING: They want to know what you're going to do to help them.

Look, what was in Graham Platner's past was all very well known to the people that recruited him. And the party activists that did recruit him said that none of those things that they found, the Reddit post, the Nazi tattoo, all that stuff, none of it should keep him from the nomination.

CORNISH: Yes. They were a Democratic Party but socialists.

DOVERE: But, importantly, no, the --

CORNISH: Well, I'm sorry, can we talk about recruitment for a second?

CORNISH: That means something here. Who recruited him? And is this a lesson learned for the people who are out there saying the parties aren't doing it too well. It's time for other people to step in and do recruiting.

DOVERE: The people who recruited him were people from -- who were not from Maine, for the most part. And those are the same people who are now saying that they get to decide what happens here. One of the top consultants for Platner was in Europe over the last couple days. He is now flying to Maine to help the process that they think that they are going to dictate of who gets to be Platner's replacement.

And again, I do think it's an interesting choice to say that this man, who is accused of sexual assault, now gets to put his will onto the party. SCHILLING: Can I just -- my main point was, is that all that stuff was

already well known. But what changed here, I think, is what these people are seeing on camera, where the voters are saying, we can't support him anymore. And it's not so much with the allegations. I think that just the dynamics of the race have changed. And I think what's worrisome, if I was a Democrat, what I would be worried about right now is it seems that more and more these Democratic Party primaries are no longer primaries and elections, they're just suggestion boxes. And the elites really don't like suggestions.

CORNISH: Let me talk about the suggestion, because now we have to figure out who is next. What is the process? You've got some people jockeying for position. But in the meantime, you've got them just saying basically, beat Susan Collins. We've got a couple names there. Most of them are people who hold state office or have run for state office. But here's a sample of who's talking.

TROY JACKSON, FORMER MAINE SENATE PRESIDENT: Short timeline. Obviously money and all that is going to be a challenge. But I definitely think it's doable because the message, the movement is right and that's what's important.

SHENNA BELLOWS, MAINE SECRETARY OF STATE: I think it's really important that as many Democratic voters have a voice in this process when Graham Platner withdraws.

DR. NIRAV SHAH (D), FORMER MAINE GOVERNOR CANDIDATE: The stakes are incredibly high. We have an obligation, as -- in service of not just Mainers, but in service of the entire country to defeat Senator Collins this upcoming November.

CORNISH: And working backwards, that was Dr. Nirav Shah, former Maine governor candidate, Shenna Bellows, the secretary of state, and Troy Jackson, former Maine Senate president.

For people who might have some trauma around the Biden dropping out of the race and the air quotes process that followed, there are a lot of questions. Should the party have a pop-up convention on the weekend of the 25th, which is the deadline to choose a nominee, should they have a statewide caucus, basically redo the primary election?

Antjuan, what do you think they should do?

SEAWRIGHT: I think we should follow the process.

CORNISH: There is no process.

SEAWRIGHT: Well, that's not true. There is a process in place to put a nominee into place. And I think that's what happened in 2024, regardless of how you -- how you -- whether you agree with how Kamala Harris became my party's nominee. I think we have to follow process.

CORNISH: What is the process? I'm missing something.

SEAWRIGHT: Maine has a process in place to replace a person on their ballot. I think it's like a party pick kind of situation.

SEAWRIGHT: It's a party pick because there is a process in place. There are process in place all across the country for these emergency situations that happen.

I think we need to follow that. That's number one.

Number two, I also don't want to give Republicans a slot here in this moment because they have just as corrupt candidates, from Paxton and others who are on the ballot, but they do not govern themselves the same way when it comes to their issues and Democratic issues. And I think there's a lesson for Democrats to learn in these moments.

Look at Ken Paxton on July 4th. And now we're looking at Ken Paxton. I mean we're looking at our candidate in Maine. There's a lesson about how these things are covered and how we talk about them within the party.

DOVERE: But the lesson for some Democrats was what was going on until Monday with Platner, which is to say all of those things that are supposed to be problems we're going to say aren't problems and we should go forward here. And that is part of how the Democrats got to this place.

And it's a difference in how Democrats and Republicans operate for sure, whether it's Ken Paxton or Max Miller or there are a lot of -- or Donald Trump, there are a lot of people --

SEAWRIGHT: That's right.

DOVERE: Who are Republicans who have had a lot of accusations made against them that are pretty serious.

SCHILLING: But again, the reason that Paxton's succeeding is because he has a compelling policy agenda. He won over the voters. He had 130 --

SCHILLING: Antjuan, he had $130 million in party money spent against him, and he crushed it by -- won by double digits.

SEAWRIGHT: Terry, Terry, Terry. Terry --

SCHILLING: It's about -- it's about substance. It's about policy. It's about how you're going to protect people and make their lives better, period. SEAWRIGHT: Terry, Terry, respectfully, you're going to ignore the

SCHILLING: There's no -- there's no rape allegations. There's no Nazi tattoos. There's no reckless (ph) --

SEAWRIGHT: You -- no, no, you're going to -- you're going to ignore the moral conversation around Ken Paxton?

SCHILLING: No, no. No, here's the moral --

SEAWRIGHT: You're going to ignore the moral conversation around Max Miller beating his wife in front of his kids.

SCHILLING: Here's the moral conversation. Here's the -- here's the moral conversation in a democracy.

SEAWRIGHT: You can't -- you can't come to us and say that these things --

SCHILLING: In a democracy voters get to decide.

SEAWRIGHT: Somehow or another we should ignore those things because of somehow or another some right-wing policy issues --

SCHILLING: In a democracy, voters get to choose their party leader (ph).

CORNISH: All right, I'm going to bring it back to a female victim. I want to just repeat this before we go. This is Lyndsey Fifield, who is the accuser against Graham Platner, talking about violence and abuse. And she says something that I think we should remember going forward as we're trying to decide politically, as a political class, what matters in a man's history. And she says, "the things that he does in private, in his relationships with women, the things that he has done to women, coercive, control, lying, deception, abuse, and really horrible things that we know about him," she says, "we know that you cannot be that way in private and not have it translate into how you would govern."

You guys, stay with us. We're going to come back.

Right now I want to show you live images from New York because they are actually trying to stabilize a high rise. Crews are working overnight to shore up the weakest parts of a building.

SCOTT JENNINGS, CNN SENIOR POLITICAL COMMENTATOR: We talk for about 17 minutes. You know, he talked a lot about the situation with Iran. He talked a little bit about Ukraine and what's going on in Europe. I'd gotten a few text messages from him, but I hadn't spoken to him.

So, it was good to hear his voice. His voice sounded strong and sounded like he was keeping up with the news to me.

CORNISH: OK, Senator Mitch McConnell has been hospitalized for three weeks. Still unclear why or when he'll be released. And yet some MAGA voices demanding, quote, "proof of life." Here's an example. Laura Loomer posting, "high level source close to the White House tells me, quote, 'Mitch McConnell is officially brain dead and he's not coming back'."

Again, that is Laura Loomer on X saying that.

And then this is a former senior adviser for Kamala Harris' campaign, Mike Nellis.

MIKE NELLIS, FORMER SENIOR ADVISER FOR KAMALA HARRIS' CAMPAIGN: If Scott Jennings and Thune and everybody else could have a 20 minute phone call with Mitch McConnell, he should be able to shoot a 60 second proof of life video to show us that he's OK.

I think they know the deadline for a special election is August 3rd. If he's dead or if he's on a ventilator, if he's not able to do his job, he should resign right now. They should have a special election.

CORNISH: So, you have a lot of Senate Republicans coming out. We're going to just show some on screen, Isaac, all saying, I talked to him, I talked to him, I talked to him for 20 minutes, I talked to him for 45 minutes.

We heard -- we're hearing these pundits talk not just about his potential health, but what they think the issue is, a special election deadline.

DOVERE: Yes. Look, Audie, if you were -- hadn't been on air for three weeks and there was no answers where you had gone, probably CNN would be interested to know what was going on with you.

CORNISH: Yes, although Reddit would say thank you.

DOVERE: Same thing with -- sure. Maybe.

DOVERE: I think the same would be true of me probably for you guys, too.

DOVERE: Mitch McConnell is entitled to private information about his health, but he is a public official. He is the taxpayers of Kentucky and of the federal government -- and of the country, because he's a federal employee, are entitled to know what's going on.

SEAWRIGHT: Isaac, this is the second Republican who we've had these medical mishaps, if you will, missing in action. And I just think that from the party who claims transparency and accountability, we should just know more when these situations pop up. We had a member of Congress who was missing in action for months.

SEAWRIGHT: And we didn't hear anything. And we later learned depression. Now we have Senator McConnell. If this were Democrats, Republicans would be wetting their diapers about accountability and responsibility.

DOVERE: If these are -- if these are important jobs, as they are --

SCHILLING: Well, it's just interesting because I am old enough to remember the Biden years where we had our secretary of defense absolutely AWOL, went out for a surgery for, I think, three weeks. We had the president of the United States obviously suffering from some type of a mental affliction and suffering, dementia (ph).

SEAWRIGHT: OK, do you have medical proof of that?

SCHILLING: Well, I saw it on TV. I saw it would my own eyes.

CORNISH: Well, hold on. Let me get -- let me bring you one other thing.

SCHILLING: But we were gaslit and told that he was at the top of his game.

CORNISH: We're going to give the last word to Lloyd Austin, who you just mentioned.

SCHILLING: Here we go.

CORNISH: Because back in 2024, he answered questions about this. And this is a great way to end the show. Here is the lesson he took away from being private about your health and not telling the public.

LLOYD AUSTIN, FORMER BIDEN SECRETARY OF DEFENSE: I don't think it's news that I'm a pretty private guy. I never liked burdening others with my problems. It's just not my way.

But I've learned from this experience. Taking this kind of job means losing some of the privacy that most of us expect. The American people have a right to know if their leaders are facing health challenges that might affect their ability to perform their duties, even temporarily.

CORNISH: And later on, testified before Congress that there would be changes made to notify the chain of command, as well as the public. There is a way for Congress and lawmakers to deal with this. They have not chosen to.

I want to thank you guys for being here. I want to thank you for waking up with me. I'm Audie Cornish, and the headlines are next.

vocabulary.excerpt: We did see last night the president ordering up those retaliatory strikes on Iran.
